planting.note: Direct sow after soil warms in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; hairy grama is one of the most drought-tolerant native grasses of the Great Plains and Southwest, tolerating more rocky and shallow soils than blue grama and extending into drier, rockier habitats.

directions: Direct sow after soil warms in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; hairy grama is closely related to blue grama (Bouteloua gracilis) and often grows with it across the shortgrass prairies, mixed-grass prairies, and desert grasslands of the Great Plains and Southwest. Distinguished from blue grama by the conspicuous long awns (bristles) projecting beyond the tips of the seed head spikelets, giving the seed head a hairy or bushy appearance (hence 'hirsuta' = hairy). Like blue grama, the seed heads are held horizontally — parallel to the ground rather than upright — in the characteristic grama-grass posture. More tolerant of rocky, shallow, gravelly, and disturbed soils than blue grama, often colonizing road cuts, eroded slopes, and rocky outcrops where blue grama struggles. Important warm-season grass for dryland seed mixes, native lawn conversions, and erosion control on dry, rocky slopes across the Great Plains and Southwest. Highly palatable to livestock and deer when green.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky, sandy or clay-loam, lean soil in full sun; galleta grass spreads readily by rhizomes once established, forming a dense sod. Can be difficult to establish from seed — seeding into moist seed beds immediately after rains or irrigation gives best results.

directions: Direct sow in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky, sandy or clay-loam, lean soil in full sun; galleta grass is the dominant sod-forming native grass of the Colorado Plateau, Navajo Nation lands, and Great Basin desert — the characteristic grass of the wide, flat, hard, often alkaline or clay-rich desert floors and mesa tops of the intermountain West. Spreads aggressively by rhizomes to form dense, low-growing turf that suppresses weeds and resists erosion on dry, hard-packed ground. The wiry, stiff stems and semi-rolled leaves are adaptations to the extreme drought, cold, wind, and alkaline conditions of the Colorado Plateau. An essential component of Great Basin desert restoration seed mixes for reclaiming disturbed desert soils, roadsides, and mine spoils. The deep rhizome network provides exceptional erosion control on dry, exposed slopes. Highly important as forage for livestock, pronghorn, and mule deer across the vast piñon-juniper and sagebrush rangelands.

planting.note: Direct sow in late spring in very well-drained to seasonally wet, slightly alkaline to neutral, heavy clay or fine sandy loam soil in full sun; tobosa grass grows naturally in Chihuahuan Desert playas (dry lake beds) and swales that receive concentrated runoff, adapting to both extreme drought between rains and brief periodic flooding.

directions: Direct sow in late spring in very well-drained to seasonally wet, slightly alkaline to neutral, heavy clay or fine sandy loam soil in full sun; tobosa grass is the dominant warm-season grass of the Chihuahuan Desert playa flats and desert swales — the wide, flat, internally-draining dry lake beds and seasonally wet depressions of the Chihuahuan Desert from west Texas to southern New Mexico and northern Chihuahua. A coarse, stiff, rhizomatous warm-season grass that forms distinctive, dense, nearly pure-stand tobosa flats on heavy clay soils that flood briefly after summer monsoon rains and then dry completely. The stiff, curling leaves are unpalatable to livestock when dry and mature, but the green growth after summer rains is important forage. Very tolerant of the extreme conditions of playa-flat habitats — alternating drought, heat, and brief flooding on dense, shrinking-and-cracking Vertisol clay soils. An essential species for the restoration of degraded Chihuahuan Desert grassland playa habitats. More heat-tolerant than galleta grass, extending into the hotter Chihuahuan and Sonoran desert zones.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; bush muhly grows naturally beneath desert shrubs and in rocky desert washes in the Chihuahuan and Sonoran deserts, often found sheltering under desert shrubs (mesquite, acacia, creosote). Extremely heat and drought tolerant once established.

directions: Direct sow in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; bush muhly is one of the most distinctive and graceful native grasses of the Sonoran and Chihuahuan deserts — a wiry-stemmed, fine-leaved bunchgrass with a uniquely open, spreading, almost shrubby growth habit (unlike the tight tufts of most other grasses) and feathery, airy, purple-tinged flower and seed panicles that are extremely ornamental in late summer and fall. The open, branching, almost woody-at-the-base stems spread outward and become partially supported by nearby desert shrubs, earning the common name 'bush muhly.' In the autumn flowering and seeding season, the fine-textured purple-pink panicles create a misty, ethereal effect highly attractive in ornamental and naturalistic plantings. One of the best native ornamental grasses for the hot, dry Southwest garden — deer-resistant, drought-tolerant, extremely heat-hardy. Widely used in desert-adapted landscape plantings in Arizona, New Mexico, and Texas. A favorite of hummingbirds for the purple-flowering form.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained to seasonally wet, alkaline to neutral, clay-loam to sandy loam, lean to average soil in full sun; alkali sacaton is the most alkaline- and salt-tolerant of the native southwestern bunchgrasses, growing on saline flats, alkali playas, and seasonally flooded bottomlands where most grasses cannot survive.

directions: Direct sow in spring in well-drained to seasonally wet, alkaline to neutral, clay-loam to sandy loam, lean to average soil in full sun; alkali sacaton is one of the most ecologically important and visually striking native grasses of the arid and semi-arid Southwest — a large, coarse bunchgrass producing massive, open, airy panicles (up to 20 inches long) of extraordinarily fine-textured, wind-trembling seed clusters that light up golden in morning and evening light. The enormous panicle creates one of the most beautiful seed head effects of any native grass — a luminous, cloud-like mass of fine-branched flower stalks hovering above the blue-green foliage in summer. The primary ecological niche is alkaline, saline, and seasonally flooded low-lying sites — alkali flats, salt meadows, river floodplains, and irrigation-influenced soils — where its exceptional tolerance for salt and alkali makes it the dominant or sole grass. An outstanding ornamental grass for challenging alkaline soils of the arid West. Very fast-germinating and establishing from seed. Related to prairie dropseed (S. heterolepis) but much larger.
